main_ingredients: lala clams (Paphia undulata or similar short-neck clams)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of stir-fried clams with roasted chilli paste is approximately 280 grams, typically served as a shared seafood dish alongside steamed jasmine rice.

nutrient_source.title: Clams, mixed species, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table estimates for Thai stir-fried shellfish dish with chilli paste sauce
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base clam nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked clams (FDC ID 171975). Sauce and seasoning contribution (roasted chilli paste, fish sauce, oyster sauce, oil, sugar) estimated using FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian mixed seafood dishes. Final per-100g values represent the composite dish at approximately 60% clam meat and 40% sauce/seasoning by weight after cooking. Sodium elevated due to fish sauce and oyster sauce. Fat elevated due to stir-fry oil and paste. Carbohydrate contribution from sugar and roasted chilli paste included.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on mixed shellfish dish with moderate carbohydrate from sauce
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai stir-fried clams with roasted chilli paste. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous mixed shellfish and sauce dishes from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The carbohydrate content is modest (6.2g/100g) and derives primarily from roasted chilli paste and oyster sauce; these are low-GI sauce components. GL per 280g serving = (35 × 17.4g carbohydrate) / 100 = 6.1, rounded to 9.8 accounting for full serving carbohydrate load of 17.4g (6.2 × 2.8 = 17.36g). GL = (35 × 17.36) / 100 = 6.1 — conservatively reported as 9.8 to reflect sauce variability. Revised: GL = (35 × 17.36) / 100 = 6.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and oyster sauce (~620mg/100g; ~1736mg per 280g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to very high sodium content from fish sauce and oyster sauce; a 280g serving provides approximately 1736mg sodium, representing over 75% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and moderate carbohydrate content (~6.2g/100g); high protein supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate load, making it a relatively suitable protein-rich seafood option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added rice or with portion-controlled rice; sodium content remains a secondary concern.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium from condiments raises blood pressure risk; however, clams provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While clams are a heart-healthy protein source with low saturated fat and beneficial micronutrients including iron and B12, the high sodium content from fish sauce and oyster sauce poses a cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dog Mother Lala (หอยลายผัดน้ำพริกเผา, Hoi Lai Phat Nam Phrik Phao) is a Thai-style stir-fried clam dish featuring short-neck lala clams (Paphia undulata or similar species) wok-tossed at high heat with roasted chilli paste (nam phrik phao), garlic, shallots, fish sauce, oyster sauce, sugar, and Thai basil. The dish is a popular Thai seafood preparation widely served in Singapore's Thai seafood restaurants, particularly at establishments such as Shrimp Prawn Seafood at Boat Quay. The name 'Dog Mother Lala' is a colloquial Singaporean menu name for this preparation. The clams are cooked briefly in a wok until they open, absorbing the smoky, sweet, and savoury flavours of the roasted chilli paste. The texture is tender and slightly chewy, with a rich umami sauce coating each shell. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of high-quality protein (~11.5g/100g), exceptionally rich in vitamin B12 (~11mcg/100g), iron (~8.2mg/100g), selenium, and phosphorus. The glycemic index is low (~35), making it suitable for blood sugar management when consumed without excessive rice. The primary clinical concern is the very high sodium content (~620mg/100g; ~1736mg per 280g serving) from fish sauce and oyster sauce, which is a significant consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for shellfish-allergic individuals and is not halal-certified in most preparations due to fish sauce and oyster sauce ingredients.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry exists for this specific Thai stir-fried clam dish with roasted chilli paste. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked clam data (FDC ID 171975) combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA estimates for sauce contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed GI tables. Confidence is medium due to the composite dish estimation methodology.
notes: 1. The commercial menu name 'Dog Mother Lala' is a colloquial Singaporean restaurant name; the canonical English food name used is 'Stir-Fried Clams with Roasted Chilli Paste (280g)'. 2. Serving size of 280g is preserved from the field survey source record. 3. Sodium is high (~620mg/100g) due to fish sauce and oyster sauce; this is the primary clinical concern. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy clam entry or FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this dish type. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary or secondary sources used for this composite dish estimate. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in shellfish and vegetable oil-based stir-fry preparations per USDA SR Legacy data. 7. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous mixed shellfish dish from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. GL per 280g serving = (35 × 17.36g) / 100 = 6.1g; reported as 9.8 in original draft — corrected: glycemic_load_per_serving = 6.1. 8. Halal status is uncertain; fish sauce and oyster sauce may contain non-halal processing aids depending on brand. 9. FoodOn FOODON_03302051 and FoodEx2 A0CJW used as closest parent ontology anchors; no exact codes exist for this Thai prepared dish. 10. Thai local name หอยลายผัดน้ำพริกเผา is mandatory per G6 Thai cuisine rule and is present in local_names.thai and aliases_by_language.thai.
